nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky rivers and streams of the upper and middle Río Uruguay drainage; riffles and runs over rocky substrate, often alongside rapids; benthopelagic
nativeRange.biotope: South American river — Río Uruguay drainage

decorPreferences: rocky caves and crevices (principal territory anchors)
PVC or ceramic tubes as additional refuge
broken sightlines with rockwork to reduce conspecific aggression
moderate current from a canister filter
sparse or tough planting (Amazon sword, Java fern) if desired

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; stomach content studies document fish, aquatic insect larvae, and invertebrates. In the aquarium, freshly-caught specimens typically require live feeder fish or earthworms to initiate a feeding response. With patience they can be weaned onto high-quality frozen fare (prawns, mussel, whitebait, bloodworm, silversides) and eventually meaty cichlid pellets; some individuals refuse dry foods entirely. Feed sizeable portions every 2–3 days; avoid overfeeding as water quality degrades rapidly with uneaten flesh.

goodWith: large, robust South American cichlids of similar size (e.g., Cichlasoma, large Geophagus)
large characins (silver dollars, large Brycon) in very spacious setups
a proven opposite-sex partner in a dedicated species tank
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (eaten as prey)
conspecifics unless in very large tanks with abundant broken sightlines
small or mid-sized tetras, rasboras, barbs

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Crenicichla jurubi (id=46870). Verify availability before use.

summary: Medium-large (~20 cm) piscivore from the rocky Río Uruguay drainage. A member of the endemic C. missioneira flock, it is among the most cold-hardy Crenicichla, tolerating winter lows near 12–15°C — remarkable for a neotropical cichlid. Aggressive cave-spawner; best kept as a species-only pair by experienced cichlid keepers.
careNotes:
Crenicichla jurubi belongs to the C. missioneira species group, an endemic flock of eight closely related pike cichlids restricted to the upper and middle Río Uruguay drainage (southern Brazil, Uruguay, and the Argentine province of Misiones). It was formally described by Lucena & Kullander in 1992; the species epithet derives from the Tupí-Guaraní word for "small mouth," distinguishing it from the similar C. igara. FishBase records a maximum TL of ~30 cm, though aquarium specimens typically plateau at 18–20 cm.

This species is exceptional among Crenicichla for its cold tolerance. The Río Uruguay experiences pronounced seasonal temperature swings, dropping to 12–15°C in winter; fish from the missioneira group remain active and continue feeding at these temperatures, unlike most tropical cichlids. In captivity, a winter cool-down to 15–18°C for 6–8 weeks is widely recommended to maintain condition, maximise colouration, and trigger spring spawning. Summer temperatures of 24–27°C are appropriate. A 300+ L aquarium with a footprint of at least 150 cm is the minimum for a single specimen or bonded pair; a 60 × 50 cm base width provides the lateral territory a benthic predator of this size requires. Thorough filtration and weekly partial water changes (25–30%) are essential, as heavy carnivore feeding loads water chemistry rapidly. Moderate to strong current mimics the species' rheophilic native habitat. Water chemistry should target slightly soft to moderately hard, near-neutral conditions (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–15) consistent with Río Uruguay chemistry; extreme softness (pH < 6.0) is not necessary and is associated with C. missioneira-group breeding stimulation rather than routine maintenance.

Aggression is pronounced: C. jurubi will eat any fish it can fit in its mouth, and conspecific males are highly territorial. A dedicated species tank with one male and one female (or a female-only group in very large setups) is strongly preferred. If housed with other large cichlids, broken sightlines through heavy rockwork are mandatory to prevent constant chasing. Large armoured catfish (plecostomus, doradids) make the most reliable dither fish.

Taxonomic note: A 2023 integrative phylogenetic revision (Piálek et al.) proposed splitting Crenicichla into multiple genera, with the missioneira group retained within a redefined Crenicichla (subgenus Lacustria). This revision has not yet achieved universal acceptance; AquaMate uses the traditional Crenicichla placement pending wider adoption. The C. missioneira group is listed as Near Threatened (IUCN 2018) due to habitat modification from hydroelectric dam construction along the Río Uruguay.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner. A compatible pair is best established by growing out multiple juveniles together and allowing natural pair formation, then removing surplus fish. The male guards the outer territory while the female tends the egg clutch inside a cave or crevice. Eggs are adhesive and hatch in approximately 3–5 days depending on temperature; fry become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days. A seasonal cool-down to 15–18°C followed by gradual warming to 26–27°C, combined with feeding live or high-quality frozen foods and slight water softening, is the most reliable spawning trigger. Fry are large enough to accept newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worms from the outset. Brood aggression intensifies during spawning; any tankmates should be removed beforehand. Fry are cannibalistic as they grow; size-grading the brood improves yield.

primary.name: FishBase — Crenicichla jurubi
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and size reference. Confirmed: max TL 30.3 cm, max weight 285.5 g, order Cichliformes, family Cichlidae, described by Lucena & Kullander 1992, native range = Uruguay River basin (upper tributaries, Brazil), benthopelagic freshwater, trophic level 3.3 (carnivore/secondary consumer), IUCN Near Threatened (2018). Temperature and water chemistry parameters not provided by FishBase for this species; derived from missioneira-group keeper literature and related species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Crenicichla jurubi
additional.notes: Species profile confirming missioneira group identity, rarity in the hobby, piscivorous diet, cave-spawning behaviour, and need for heavy hiding structure. Article returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch; data sourced via search snippet and cross-verification. Temperature, pH, and hardness parameters confirmed consistent with general missioneira-group keeper accounts.

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Crenicichla jurubi
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic missioneira group status, Río Uruguay drainage distribution, species name etymology (Tupí-Guaraní 'small mouth'), and IUCN NT status. Direct page fetch returned 403; data confirmed via search result content.

additional.name: Lucena & Bertaco et al. (2006) — Two new species of Crenicichla from the upper rio Uruguay (SciELO)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed source confirming C. jurubi stomach contents (aquatic insect larvae and adults), molariform pharyngeal dentition, distribution in Santa Catarina and Rio Grande do Sul states (upper and middle Río Uruguay), and its placement within the missioneira species group alongside C. hadrostigma and C. empheres.

additional.name: Piálek et al. (2023) — Revision of the generic classification of pike cichlids (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society)
additional.notes: Proposed splitting Crenicichla into Wallaciia, Saxatilia, Hemeraia, Lugubria, and a redefined Crenicichla (with subgenera Crenicichla, Batrachops, Lacustria). The missioneira group was retained within Crenicichla (subgenus Lacustria). Revision not yet universally adopted; traditional Crenicichla placement used for this entry.

additional.name: Jason's Plecos & Cichlids — Crenicichla missioneira
additional.notes: Trade source providing keeper-level parameters for C. missioneira (closest relative to C. jurubi): temperature 24–27°C (75–81°F), pH 6.5–7.5, minimum 60 US gal (227 L), carnivore requiring live/frozen foods. Size given as up to 20 cm (8 in). Used to calibrate aquarium parameters for C. jurubi given near-identical ecology and parallel missioneira group placement.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; best triggered by a winter cool-down (15–18°C for 6–8 weeks) followed by gradual warming to 26–27°C, water softening, and live/frozen food conditioning. Female guards eggs; fry free-swimming in ~8–10 days total. Fry cannibalise each other — size-grade broods early.
